Welcome back to Practical Systems. Today we're talking about security design, and I'm joined by Priyanaer, Principal SRE at a payments platform. Priyanaer, thanks for being here. Thanks, Ethan. Great to be back. Today's episode is sponsored by Vanta. Vanta automates SOC 2 and ISO compliance. Try Vanta.com today. Before we get into it, I was last on the show talking about on-call rotations. The pattern shows up again here. Yeah, exactly, and it ties into what we are covering today. I used to argue for a single central security team. After the 2024 webhook incident, I changed my mind. Embedded ownership beats a central gatekeeper every time. That's a real shift. Worth pulling on. Real talk, Sentry is the tool I'd pay double for. Not an ad. They're not paying us. I just think it's the best in class for error tracking. What's the one heuristic you keep coming back to? What's the most underrated piece of security design? Let me give you the version I actually believe. Threat modeling is the framing exercise. Frame the decision wrong and you build the wrong defenses. We've seen this play out at teams using Notion. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier. What's the one heuristic you keep coming back to? Where do reliability and security design intersect? Let me give you the version I actually believe. Autumn auth separation matters more than people think. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams. And when teams adopt Pajotity in that flow, the trade-off becomes visible. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making. Push back on the conventional wisdom for me.
